did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and machine learning for melanoma skin cancer detection. Specifically, it employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to extract deep features, Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) for handcrafted local features, Semisupervised Discriminant Analysis (SDA) for feature reduction, and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier optimized by a Bayesian optimizer. The method was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents a novel method for melanoma detection involving the fusion of deep and time-frequency local features, describes specific methodologies (CNNs, SIFT, SDA, SVM), details the use of the HAM10000 dataset, and reports original experimental results including accuracy (94.19%) and sensitivity (96.22%). While it includes a literature review section, the primary purpose is to report original experimental findings.

title: Fusion of Deep and Time–Frequency Local Features for Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection
journal: Applied Computational Intelligence and Soft Computing
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel method for melanoma skin cancer detection by fusing deep features extracted from convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with handcrafted time-frequency local features derived from Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) and two-dimensional discrete wavelet transform (2D DWT). The highly correlated features are reduced using semisupervised discriminant analysis (SDA), with its parameters and the Gaussian kernel of the support vector machine (SVM) classifier optimized via a Bayesian optimizer. The proposed framework was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which includes dermoscopic images of seven common skin lesions. Simulation results demonstrate that the method achieves an accuracy of 94.19% and a sensitivity of 96.22%. The findings indicate that integrating deep and local features yields higher accuracy than using them separately, addressing the challenge of improving detection precision.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the proposed detection method. The paper focuses on distinguishing melanoma from other skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset used for evaluation. Described as irregular and scaly patches on the skin due to sun exposure, assumed to be pre-cancerous.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as appearing as sore that seems to heal, then comes back and bleeds.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset. Referred to as 'benign keratosis-like lesions' in the context of the dataset classes.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a benign fibrous nodule, or cutaneous fibrous histiocytoma, commonly occurring on the lower legs.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as melanocyte-derived hamartoma or benign neoplasm and pigment-generating cells (moles).
Included in the HAM10000 dataset. Described as tumors that can appear anywhere on the body and structures can be benign or malignant, often called birthmarks.

all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on binary classification for melanoma skin cancer detection. The abstract states the method is for 'MEL detection' and the results section explicitly mentions characterizing 'MEL and non-MEL images' and calculating sensitivity for MEL and correct classification rate for non-MEL images. The SVM classifier is optimized to maximize classification accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin lesion classes (AKIEC, BCC, BLS, DF, NV, VASC, MEL)
all_task_types.details: The study utilizes the HAM10000 dataset which comprises seven common skin cancer classes. While the primary reported results are for binary melanoma detection, the dataset and CNN architectures (DenseNet201, VGG19, Xception) are described in the context of classifying these seven classes, with the final fully connected layer modified according to the number of classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 10,015 dermatoscopic images of seven common skin lesions, used to assess the performance of the proposed melanoma detection method.

use.training: Used for training the proposed framework with data augmentation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the proposed method, reporting accuracy and sensitivity.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the performance metrics by partitioning the dataset into five nonoverlapping parts, using one part as test data and the others for training, repeating the procedure five times, and averaging the results.
To calculate and report sensitivity, precision, and F1-score for the classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma classes.
To find the optimum parameters of the semisupervised discriminant analysis (SDA) and the Gaussian kernel of the support vector machine (SVM) classifier to maximize classification accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and confusion matrix analysis via five-fold cross-validation.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid framework fusing deep features from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and handcrafted time-frequency local features (SIFT and 2D DWT), processed by Semisupervised Discriminant Analysis (SDA) and classified by a Support Vector Machine (SVM).
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was utilized for the CNNs (DenseNet201, VGG19, and Xception).
architecture_used.input_size: Input images were resized to the input layer size of each specific CNN using bicubic interpolation. DenseNet201 input size is not explicitly stated but implied by architecture; VGG19 and Xception typically use 224x224 or similar, but the text specifies the HAM10000 images are 600x450 pixels before resizing. The Xception architecture diagram shows an input of 224x224.

DenseNet201_layers: Transition layer (1) (2x2 average pooling, stride 2, 1x1 conv)
Transition layer (2) (2x2 average pooling, stride 2, 1x1 conv)
Transition layer (3) (2x2 average pooling, stride 2, 1x1 conv)
architecture_details.Xception_architecture_notes: Includes 3x3 conv, 3x3 separable conv (sconv), 1x1 conv, 3x3 max pool, filter concat, global avg pool, and FC layers. Input size shown in Figure 3 is 224x224.

local_feature_extraction.method: Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) and Two-Dimensional Discrete Wavelet Transform (2D DWT)
local_feature_extraction.DWT_details: One-level 2D DWT decomposes the lesion area into four subbands: approximation (A1), horizontal (H1), vertical (V1), and diagonal (D1).
local_feature_extraction.SIFT_details: Extracted from the four subbands of the one-level 2D DWT. Involves scale-space generation, extreme point detection, direction amplitude calculation, and key point description.

architecture_details.feature_fusion: Concatenation of deep feature vectors (y_deep) and local feature vectors (y_local) to form final feature vector y_f.
architecture_details.feature_reduction: Semisupervised Discriminant Analysis (SDA) to reduce highly correlated and redundant features. Parameters optimized via Bayesian optimizer.
architecture_details.classifier: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Gaussian kernel. Parameters optimized via Bayesian optimizer.

training_details.optimizer_for_cnn: Transfer learning (specific optimizer not stated, likely standard for pretrained models)
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly stated for CNNs; SVM maximizes classification accuracy via Gaussian kernel optimization.
training_details.data_augmentation: Data augmentation was applied to the HAM10000 dataset. Specific augmentations mentioned include zooms in range (0.8, 1.2) and three random rotations between 0 and 90 degrees.

classification_or_output_head.details: The last fully connected layer of the CNNs was modified according to the number of classes in the work (binary). The final classifier is an SVM.

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization by eliminating red, green, and blue channel values (conversion to grayscale)
Data augmentation for the melanoma class: horizontal and vertical flips, random zooms in range (0.8, 1.2), and random rotations between 0 and 90 degrees
Image resizing to 256 × 256 pixels using bicubic interpolation
One-level 2D Discrete Wavelet Transform (2D DWT) using Daubechies four filters to decompose images into approximation, horizontal, vertical, and diagonal subbands
Gamma correction with gamma values of 0.7, 1.3, and 1.7
Sigmoid correction applied to melanoma images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the melanoma (MEL) class to balance the dataset.
Applied to the melanoma (MEL) class with a range of (0.8, 1.2).
Applied to the melanoma (MEL) class with three random rotations between 0 and 90 degrees.

conclusions: The proposed method, which fuses deep features from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with handcrafted time-frequency local features (SIFT and 2D DWT), achieves a classification accuracy of 94.19% and a sensitivity of 96.22% for melanoma detection.
Integrating deep and local features yields higher accuracy than using them separately, with the combination of VGG19, DenseNet201, and Xception deep features providing the maximum accuracy.
Using a Gaussian kernel Support Vector Machine (G-SVM) classifier results in higher accuracy compared to a linear kernel SVM (L-SVM) across all combinations of deep and local features.
Data augmentation has a more significant impact on the performance of the proposed method than parameter optimization, particularly affecting sensitivity due to the unbalanced nature of the dataset.
Semisupervised Discriminant Analysis (SDA) is effective for reducing highly correlated and redundant features, balancing generalization and discrimination.
The most challenging aspects of the proposed method are the extraction of deep features and the tuning of parameters for SDA and the Gaussian-SVM classifier.
Using only local features yields lower accuracy (72.29% with L-SVM and 73.81% with G-SVM) compared to methods utilizing deep features, indicating that deep features considerably affect accuracy.
The correct classification rate for non-melanoma images is 93.93%, demonstrating the method's effectiveness in distinguishing melanoma from other skin lesions.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., NV has 6705 samples vs. DF with 115), potentially biasing results toward majority classes.

Data augmentation was applied only to the melanoma (MEL) class to balance the dataset, which may introduce synthetic bias and limit the generalizability of the model to real-world clinical distributions.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, limiting the assessment of statistical significance.

External validation was not performed; the model was evaluated solely using five-fold cross-validation on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The method's complexity involves multiple stages (CNN feature extraction, 2D DWT, SIFT, SDA, Bayesian optimization, SVM), making it computationally intensive and potentially difficult to deploy in clinical settings compared to end-to-end deep learning models.

The paper does not address potential data leakage risks from data augmentation techniques applied during cross-validation, which could lead to optimistic performance estimates if augmented images from the same original lesion appear in both training and test folds.

Clinical relevance is limited as the study focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using dermoscopic images, without integrating patient clinical information or histopathological validation, which are critical for comprehensive diagnosis.
